clean_story:
Ice cream sales rise. Drowning deaths spike.
The phrase "correlation" means two things happening together—but not why. Like a thermometer and sweat: both climb in summer, but one doesn’t cause the other.

Humans mistake correlation for causation when they assume "A leads to B" just because A and B often appear paired. Example: Cities with more churches have higher crime rates. Does religion cause crime? No—they’re both tied to population size. Or: Storks nest near high birthrate towns in Europe. Do storks deliver babies? No—farming villages (with stork-friendly roofs) also have young families.

The brain craves patterns, so it invents arrows where none exist. A 2012 study found 80% of people believed unrelated graphs showed causation if labeled suggestively (e.g., "Pirate attacks vs. global warming"). Even scientists fall for it: Early 1900s doctors linked masturbation to blindness—until better data arrived.

Three related subjects are spurious statistics, confirmation bias tricks, and how graphs lie silently.
